extracted_rules.condition: When establishing swim training zones for any athlete without prior CSS data
extracted_rules.action: Derive CSS from 400m and 200m all-out time trials on the same day; this pace is the aerobic threshold for swimming
extracted_rules.formula_or_value: CSS (m/s) = (D400 - D200) / (T400 - T200) where D=distance in meters, T=time in seconds; CSS pace = 100m split at that speed
extracted_rules.source: Wakayoshi et al. 1992 (original CSS derivation); swimming physiology consensus

extracted_rules.condition: If scheduling a recovery or warm-up swim
extracted_rules.action: Swim at below 80% of CSS pace; fully aerobic, minimal lactate accumulation
extracted_rules.formula_or_value: <80% CSS pace; RPE ≤ 3; conversational breathing pattern; used for warm-up, cool-down, and active recovery
extracted_rules.source: CSS zone framework derived from Wakayoshi 1992 + swimming physiology standards

extracted_rules.condition: If scheduling an aerobic base or endurance swim
extracted_rules.action: Swim at 80-94% of CSS pace; primary zone for aerobic base building; forms 70-80% of total swim volume
extracted_rules.formula_or_value: 80-94% CSS pace; RPE 4-6; sustainable for long sets; main aerobic development zone
extracted_rules.source: CSS zone framework derived from Wakayoshi 1992 + swimming physiology standards

extracted_rules.condition: If scheduling threshold or CSS-pace sets
extracted_rules.action: Swim at 95-100% of CSS pace; this is the maximal lactate steady-state; limit to ≤15% of weekly swim volume
extracted_rules.formula_or_value: 95-100% CSS pace; RPE 7-8; maximal lactate steady state; volume cap ≤15% of weekly swim time
extracted_rules.source: CSS zone framework; analogous to Daniels T-pace principle applied to swimming

extracted_rules.condition: If scheduling high-intensity interval swim sets targeting VO2max
extracted_rules.action: Swim at >100% CSS pace (race pace or faster); use short intervals with adequate rest; limit to ≤10% of weekly swim volume
extracted_rules.formula_or_value: >100% CSS pace (105-115% for VO2 sets); work:rest ratio 1:3 to 1:4; volume ≤10% weekly swim time
extracted_rules.source: CSS zone framework; VO2swim interval research

extracted_rules.action: Calculate SWOLF for each set; lower SWOLF indicates better efficiency; track trends over weeks
extracted_rules.formula_or_value: SWOLF = stroke_count_per_length + split_seconds_per_length; lower = more efficient; elite freestyle 25m: SWOLF ~30-35; recreational: 40-50
extracted_rules.source: Swimming efficiency research; SWOLF standardized metric

extracted_rules.condition: When prescribing stroke rate targets for freestyle training
extracted_rules.action: Set stroke rate targets based on distance; lower stroke rate with longer stroke for distance events, higher rate for sprint
extracted_rules.formula_or_value: Distance freestyle (1500m+): 50-60 strokes/min; middle distance (200-400m): 65-75 strokes/min; sprint (50-100m): 80-100 strokes/min
extracted_rules.source: Toussaint & Hollander 1994; competitive swimming stroke rate research

extracted_rules.rule_name: Open-water drafting — position and energy savings
extracted_rules.condition: If athlete races in open-water or triathlon swim
extracted_rules.action: Position 0-50cm directly behind the lead swimmer's feet; hip-draft position (beside lead swimmer's hips) offers partial benefit
extracted_rules.formula_or_value: Feet-draft (0-50cm behind): energy cost reduction ~11-38%; hip-draft (beside hips): ~5-15% reduction; position must be maintained actively
extracted_rules.source: Systematic review: aerodynamic drafting in mass-start non-motorized sports (original swimming_coach_biomechanics source)

extracted_rules.rule_name: Triathlon swim exit — reduce intensity pre-T1
extracted_rules.condition: If athlete is approaching the swim exit in a triathlon
extracted_rules.action: Reduce swim intensity in the final 150-200m to allow HR and breathing to stabilize before T1; prioritize a strong exit kick and breathing control
extracted_rules.formula_or_value: Final 150-200m: reduce effort to CSS Zone 2 (~85% CSS); allows HR to drop 5-10 bpm before transition; improves early bike output
extracted_rules.source: Triathlon physiology research; transition optimization consensus

extracted_rules.rule_name: Breaststroke energy cost — use for recovery only
extracted_rules.condition: If scheduling a breaststroke set for a swimmer who primarily uses freestyle
extracted_rules.action: Treat breaststroke as a recovery stroke only; its energy cost is 40% higher than freestyle at the same speed
extracted_rules.formula_or_value: Breaststroke energy cost: ~40% higher than freestyle at matched speed; active frontal drag: highest of all 4 strokes; prescribe at Zone 1 effort only
extracted_rules.source: Systematic review: variability of energy cost in breaststroke (original swimming_coach_biomechanics source)
